📺YouTube Summarizer
ホーム
The Prof G Pod – Scott Galloway

Is Imperialism Good for Your Portfolio? | Prof G Markets

2026年1月12日
▶ YouTubeで視聴

トランプの「ドンロー・ドクトリン」、銀行株高騰、そして2026年市場予測

今回のProf G Marketsでは、Financial Timesの米国金融コメンテーターであるRobert Armstrongが、司会のEd Elsonと共に、トランプ政権下の「ドンロー・ドクトリン」が市場に与える影響、銀行規制緩和による銀行株の動向、そして2026年の市場予測について深く掘り下げた。

トランプの「ドンロー・ドクトリン」と市場の反応

Donald Trump前大統領は、1823年にJames Monroe大統領が提唱した「モンロー・ドクトリン」になぞらえ、米国が西半球の支配的勢力であるとする「ドンロー・ドクトリン」を掲げている。これは、ベネズエラのニコラス・マドゥロ大統領拘束やグリーンランド買収の意向表明といった強硬な外交政策に現れた。

市場はこれらの地政学的動きに対し、全体としては無関心な反応を示した。S&Pはわずかに上昇したものの、大きな変動は見られなかった。しかし、特定の銘柄には顕著な動きがあった。ベネズエラ関連では、石油精製株のPhilip 66が6%、Valeroが12%上昇し、石油サービス株のHalliburtonやSchlumbergerも上昇した。グリーンランド関連では、レアアース企業であるCritical Metalsが25%、Energy Transition Mineralsが30%以上急騰した。一方で、ベネズエラで唯一操業する米国石油企業であるChevronの株価は、一時的な上昇の後、元の水準に戻った。これは、ベネズエラからの石油抽出が困難であるという現実を市場が認識したためと見られる。

Trumpは外交政策だけでなく、国内企業に対しても介入的な姿勢を見せている。防衛企業に対し、配当や自社株買い、高額なCEO報酬を制限するよう要求し、機関投資家による住宅購入を制限することで住宅価格高騰を抑えようとした。これらの発言は、関連企業の株価に一時的な下落をもたらしたが、その後回復した。Robert Armstrongは、市場は短期的な経済的利益を重視し、地政学的・倫理的側面を軽視する傾向があると指摘する。歴史家のPaul Kennedyが提唱する「帝国としての過剰拡大（imperial overstretch）」のリスクも存在するが、市場の投資家は短期的な視点に立ち、長期的な影響を価格に織り込んでいない。

銀行規制緩和と2025年の銀行株高騰

Trump政権下での銀行規制緩和、特に2008年の金融危機後に導入されたドッド・フランク法の一部撤廃の動きは、銀行業界に追い風となった。2025年には、大手銀行株がS&Pの2倍近い**30%**上昇し、パンデミック以降で最も好調な年となった。

この好調の背景には複数の要因がある。まず、米国経済の堅調さが銀行の貸出と預金業務を支えた。次に、規制緩和により、大手銀行の自己資本比率要件（バーゼル3最終化）が緩和され、M&Aが容易になった。さらに、高金利環境下で、銀行は低利回りの旧資産を高利回りの新資産に置き換えることで収益を改善した。例えば、2020年に3%の利回りで貸し出したローンを、2025年には7%の利回りで置き換えることができた。活発な資本市場も大手銀行の収益に貢献し、M&A取引量の40%増加や、市場のボラティリティによるトレーディング収益の15%増加が見られた。結果として、JP Morganは34%、Citigroupは**63%**もの株価上昇を記録した。

2026年も銀行業界は好調を維持すると予測されるが、株価は既に割安ではないため、昨年のような大幅な上昇は期待しにくい。Robert Armstrongは、銀行業界の統合（consolidation）が競争を促進し、投資家にとって有益であると主張する。しかし、銀行の統合は、人材流出、厳格な規制、企業文化の衝突といった課題を伴うため、実行は困難である。また、地域銀行のCEOは、自身の地位や地域社会での影響力を失いたくないため、売却に消極的である。一方で、買収側の大手銀行のCEOは、銀行規模の拡大が自身の報酬に直結するため、過払いするインセンティブがあるという、経営陣と投資家の利益相反も指摘された。

2026年の市場予測と主要リスク

Robert Armstrongは、2026年の市場について以下の予測を示した。

  • バブル: 現在の資産価格は歴史的に見て95-97パーセンタイルと高水準であり、長期的に見れば低リターンを予測する。しかし、2026年にバブルが崩壊する確率は低いと見ている。経済の堅調さ、FRBの利下げ期待、大規模な財政刺激策が、バブル崩壊の引き金となる可能性を低くしている。
  • AIの熱狂: AI株の熱狂は冷静化しつつある兆候が見られる。Nvidiaの株価は4ヶ月間横ばいで推移し、MetaやOracleも苦戦している。市場はAIの収益性についてより慎重な見方をするようになっている。OpenAIのような象徴的な企業の失速は、市場全体に大きな影響を与える可能性がある。
  • 米国株: 欧州や日本と比較して、米国の経済成長と企業収益の強さが継続するため、米国株は引き続き他国をアウトパフォームすると予測される。
  • 景気循環株: 財政刺激策と利下げ期待を背景に、景気循環株（銀行、素材、工業株など）がテクノロジー株を上回るパフォーマンスを示す可能性がある。これらは経済成長に強く連動する銘柄であり、現在の経済環境が追い風となる。
  • FRBの金利: 年末までにフェデラルファンド金利が3%を下回ることはないだろうと予測。FRB議長がTrump派になる可能性もあるが、FRBは委員会投票制であり、議長一人の影響は限定的である。
  • 最大のリスク: インフレの再燃。公的債務の増加、大規模な財政刺激策、利下げ、労働供給の減少といった要因が、インフレを再び加速させる可能性がある。最近のインフレデータは政府閉鎖の影響で不確実性が高いが、Mog Xandandyの分析では実質的なインフレ率は**3%**に留まると指摘されている。インフレが制御不能になれば、上記の全ての予測が覆される。

記事のポイント

  • 「ドンロー・ドクトリン」: トランプの強硬な外交政策は市場全体に影響を与えず、特定銘柄のみに短期的な動きが見られた。
  • 銀行株の好調: 2025年は規制緩和、高金利環境、活発な資本市場が大手銀行株を大幅に押し上げた。
  • 2026年市場予測: 資産価格は高水準だが、バブル崩壊は低確率で、AIの熱狂は冷静化し、米国株と景気循環株が優位に立つ見込み。
  • FRB金利: 年末までに3%を下回ることはなく、FRB議長交代の影響は限定的と予測される。
  • 最大のリスク: 公的債務と財政刺激策によるインフレ再燃が、2026年の市場予測を覆す最大の懸念材料である。

メタデータ

企業・組織: Financial Times, Philip 66, Valero, Critical Metals, Energy Transition Minerals, Chevron, Blackstone, Invitation Homes, JP Morgan, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, Citigroup, US Bank, BB&T, SunTrust, Nvidia, Meta, Oracle, Google, Microsoft, Anthropic, OpenAI, FTX, Fundrise, LinkedIn, Rubric, Vanta, Columbia Threadneedle, Funstrat, Barclays 人物: Robert Armstrong, Ed Elson, Donald Trump, James Monroe, Nicholas Maduro, Andy Ogles, Elizabeth Warren, Thucydides, Paul Kennedy, Kristen O'Donnie, Jamie Dimon, Tom Lee, Patrick Kaser, Dan Davies, Scott Bessent, Jerome Powell, Ed Al Husini, Paul Volcker キーワード: ドンロー・ドクトリン, モンロー・ドクトリン, 地政学, 銀行規制緩和, 銀行株, 株式市場, 2026年予測, バブル, AI, インフレ, FRB, 金利, 景気循環株, 米国経済 ワンライナー: トランプ政策と市場予測、金融コメンテーターが解説。

14:10If our only goal is to make more money,

14:14let's assume that's all we care about,

14:16should we invade nations and take their

14:20stuff? And I do think that if you're

14:23thinking in the short term and also if

14:24you're thinking in election cycles as he

14:26does there is an argument to be made

14:28that mess you that that yes you should

14:31because you know just put very simply

14:33like if I successfully rob a bank I will

14:38be richer and the other guy will be

14:40poorer and if I do it again I will get

14:42doubly as rich. But to your point, I

14:45think the trouble arises over the long

14:48term. And this is, as you say, what

14:49historians have studied for years, and

14:52that is if you overplay your hand, if

14:54you start abusing other nations to a

14:57chronic degree, if you're abandoning

15:00treaties, if you're threatening

15:01populations, it can be really bad. And

15:04this is what this historian Paul Kennedy

15:06talks about. He he calls it imperial

15:09overstretch. And that is we saw it with

15:11the Romans, we saw it with the Spanish,

15:13we even saw it with the British. You

15:15keep on trying to take take and then one

15:18by one if you kind of overstep your

15:20boundaries then suddenly you have a

15:23colony that's rebelling and then sure

15:25enough the entire thing collapses. So

15:27from a pure wealth perspective,

15:31it doesn't seem a good idea over the

15:32long term.

15:33>> Yeah, over the long term. But the the

15:35problem with these kind of arguments is

15:37you don't know when the long term is,

15:39>> right? you know, the the the investing

15:42horizon of the average investor, the

15:44time from when they have enough money

15:46that they have something to invest to

15:48the time that they retire and have to

15:50start spending the money or whatever,

15:52you know, it's you got a 20-year horizon

15:54there. You're middle-aged by the time

15:55you have anything to invest and then

15:57pretty soon you're retiring. So, like,

15:59you know, over a period of a hundred

16:01years, maybe you're right, but we're not

16:02playing a hundredyear game. Nobody in

16:04the stock market is playing. Nobody even

16:05the bond market is playing a

16:06hundred-year game game. You know what I

16:08mean? So it's it's you can understand

16:11why the market might not price these

16:15things in well because nobody in the all

16:18the people when the comeuppance comes

16:20everybody in the market today is going

16:22to be dead or in a retirement home.

16:23>> Yes.

16:24>> Exactly.

16:26>> So there we are you know

16:27>> and it explains a lot. It explains a lot

16:29of the behaviors and it almost makes me

16:35of history would tell you or not as I'm

16:38probably not doomed. You're probably not

16:40doomed. We're fine. But grandchildren,

16:42great grandchildren. I mean I even think

16:46cycle. It's like we just cannot think

16:50long term. We all we care about is the

16:53next 1 2 3 or four years. And it doesn't

16:58seem like that is a good signal. I mean,

17:01I I would like I'm basically trying to

17:05come up with a reason as to why if you

17:07had no care in the world about morality

17:10or ethics, why it would still be a bad

17:12idea to go in and steal things. That's

17:15what I'm trying to do. We had a pretty

17:25perhaps the the developed world system.

18:59in step with the way I react or you

19:03gosh, there's oil in Venezuela. Buy, oh,

19:10and cost $und00 billion to get the oil

19:47these rare earth elements and the

19:54world's metals and if you compare it to

19:57the US, we've got only 2%. And then you

20:00compare it to China, which has almost

20:0150%. And this has been such a big piece

20:11that we had leverage over China and then

20:15got this thing called rare earth

21:05as a proposition

21:07the removal of President Maduro in

25:09about guns, war, death. We're talking

25:13about very very serious things, but in a

25:16in a very unsatisfying way, it's the

25:19it's the it's the market's job and it's

25:21the Wall Street analyst's job to sort of

25:24treat it as if it's this, I don't know,

25:26an academic term or something that you

25:29write about in a research paper, which

25:31is so frustrating because it it it isn't

25:33that. There is a very long thread

25:41maybe even the 17th century that says

26:28people. It's too bad that it's not true,

26:34you know, it's just not right and we

26:37have to be reminded of it, you know,

28:27Jobs AI assistant, you can skip

29:06We're back with Profy Markets. Fourth

29:09quarter earnings season kicks off later

29:10this week with all of the big banks

29:13reporting and we'll break down the

29:14results on next Monday's episode. But

29:17today we want to zoom out and focus on

29:20the broader shift reshaping the

29:22industry. One of those shifts is

29:24deregulation. The Trump administration

29:25has made several moves to loosen banking

29:28rules. That includes rolling back parts

29:30of DoddFrank, the post 2008 framework

29:32that was designed to limit risk-taking.

29:35And the result is it is a really good

29:37time to be a bank. And we can just look

29:40back at the year 2025. the big bank

29:43stocks rose 30%

29:46over the course of the year. Uh that's

29:48nearly double the gain of the market at

29:51large, the S&P.

29:53>> So Rob, uh looking ahead to well, let's

29:57I guess start with last year, um which

30:00was the strongest year for banks since

30:02the pandemic. Um what do you make of

30:06what happened last year uh in the

30:09banking industry and what can it tell us

30:11about the year ahead? a couple of stars

30:13aligned here in a really nice way. The

30:17first and most obvious one is that banks

30:20do as well as the economy that they're

30:22sitting in. A bank, you know, and the

30:25American economy was pretty good. And

30:27that's where you have to start anytime

30:29you want to talk about banks. When the

30:31economy is strong, people borrow money,

30:34people pay back the money they borrowed

30:35from you before, all of that stuff. They

30:38deposit, they make deposits in your

30:40institution, etc. So that's good. Then

30:42you already hit the second important

30:43point which is with the election of

30:45Donald Trump the regulatory overhang

30:48lightened significantly and uh you know

30:51the most important uh for the big banks

30:54aspect of this is there were all these

30:56rules that hadn't arrived yet that they

30:58were kind of coming down the pike that

31:01were going to increase the capital

31:04requirements of the big banks even more

31:06than they already have been increased.

31:08They were of course massively increased

31:10and hardened after the financial crisis.

31:12This is to say how many dollars of

31:14actual equity capital you have to have

31:17for every dollar of loans or assets or

31:19whatever you have out there. So we were

31:22worried that what they call basil 3

31:24endgame was going to mean you know JP

31:26Morgan has to hold two more dollars in

31:29addition to the 11 or $13 they already

31:31hold for every $100 of assets. Looks

31:35less like that's going to happen. uh

31:37acquisi you know mergers are getting

31:39easier they're getting executed faster

31:41all this good stuff so regulator

31:42overhang good that is good

31:45>> now for the tricky part where listeners

31:49should either pay attention or just tune

31:50me out altogether

31:53one or the other

31:55take your pick so um back when interest

31:59rates were really low before 2022 and

32:03there was inflation and rates got higher

32:06Any asset a bank bought then has a

32:09really low yield on it. If you bought a

32:10mortgage or even a business loan or

32:1320-year Treasury, you bought it at a

32:16yield of just a couple of percent. When

32:18interest rates go up, the price of all

32:21those assets

32:23has to repric to the new interest rate,

32:26which means the value of those assets

32:28goes down. Right? So if you buy a a a

32:32mortgage bond with a yield of 2% and

32:36rates go to 4% the market value of that

32:38asset has to go down to make its yield

32:414% too. Okay. I the sound of your

32:44listeners going to sleep I I can hear it

32:46now. So that this was a very bad

32:50occasion for certain banks. It hurt a

32:51lot of banks. This is was at the root of

32:53what happened to Silicon Valley Bank

32:55last year etc. But what's happening now

32:58is that assets that were purchased back

33:02when rates were really low are rolling

33:05off of banks balance sheets. So you lose

33:07a loan you made in 2020,

33:10>> you replace it with a loan you're making

33:12in 2025. You're getting rid of something

33:15that yields 3% and replacing it with

33:18something that yields 7%.

33:20So this and that that was going on last

33:22year and it's going to go on even more

33:23this year. So there's this natural lift

33:26under banks income just as the old old

33:31world stuff rolls off and you replace it

33:34with higher yielding new stuff. So

33:36that's and final point and now readers

33:39listeners who tuned out can tune back

33:41in. Uh for the very big banks when you

33:44have very active capital markets as we

33:46had last year that's very good for your

33:48investment banking and your trading

33:50desk. So, I mean, if we were to look at

33:52what happened in 2025, all of those

33:55things that you describe, uh, leads to

33:58one of the strongest years we've ever

34:00seen for the banking industry. And by

34:02the way, just a side note, this is

34:04ironic because when Trump comes in, the

34:07whole message is the wall the guys on

34:09Wall Street have won for too long and

34:11we're going to hand it back to Main

34:12Street. It's not about getting them

34:14rich. And what do you know? I mean,

34:15everyone on Wall Street is getting

34:17richer than basically ever before.

34:19>> Than ever before. Um, I mean, let's look

34:20at just the the the big bank stocks and

37:17but you don't do that twice,

38:25that these are extremely human capital

38:27intensive businesses. Yeah, that's fair.

38:29Which could open up new opportunities

38:31for multiples. I mean, if JP Morgan

38:34decides to AI charge its business, then

38:36suddenly you have um a company that that

38:40looks more when the when you look at the

38:42business looks more like a software

38:44company than say a bank with a bunch of

38:46physical branches.

38:47>> I got two things to say about that. One,

38:49yes, high fixed costs and human capital

38:52costs are part of the reason that bank

38:54stocks traditionally trade at something

38:56of a discount to the market. The other

38:58reason is that their earnings are very

39:00volatile because they're sensitive to

39:02the economy and to the stock market and

39:04that's not going away in the AI world.

39:08multiples down. Two,

39:11>> this world in which AI gets a lot of

39:22in certain sectors, but it's going to be

39:28was just looking the other day at this

39:40regional banks, BB&T and Sunrust 5 years

39:44the merger. And they did fire the

39:47people. They had something like 58,000

39:49employees as two independent companies.

39:53more assets. So like mission

39:55accomplished on firing people, not by AI

40:45>> Yes. This um is a nice transition to uh

40:49something that you've been talking a lot

40:51about in your newsletter which I want to

40:52get your thoughts on. you've been saying

40:55that I mean just for some context

40:58we usually say on this podcast that

41:01consolidation is kind of a bad thing

41:05>> because it generally

41:08involves a monopolistic entity going in

41:11and buying up all of the smaller players

41:13which reduces competition. It leads to

41:16potentially price gouging. We generally

41:18are not huge fans of when a a big bank

41:21goes and buys or a big company goes and

41:23buys another small company. Your view

41:25that you've been talking about and I

41:26want to hear more about it is that

41:52Group, and US Bank together,

41:56you know, they're just squeezing most of

41:58the profits out of the industry and

42:01there's your igopoly,

42:02>> right?

42:03>> Right. You know what I mean? and the

42:09other banks in America that are

42:11squeezing out tiny little drops of

42:13profit uh doing local banking doesn't

42:17mean the industry is really properly

42:20competitive and from an investor point

42:23of view the small you know the regional

42:26banking industry has been a terrible

42:27place to invest and so

42:30on the I think consolidation of that is

42:34be for investors. I think it would be

42:35pro competitive for the industry. The

42:37problem is the more I read about it and

42:38the more bankers I talk to, integrating

42:41a bank merger is a nightmare.

42:45>> Why? Why is it a nightmare as opposed to

42:47anything else?

42:48>> Okay, so one reason is because it's such

42:51a human capital industry. So, the first

42:52thing that happens when BB&T and Sunrust

42:57get together is that uh everybody at the

43:02competing banks calls up the best banker

43:05at the merging banks and is like, "You

43:07really want to put up with three years

43:09of [ __ ] while these guys screw

43:10around changing their name and re-upping

43:13the why don't you come work for me

43:14instead?" And that bank that banker

43:17maybe it's a a commercial banker who

43:19makes loans to banks or it's somebody a

43:21trader whatever you know they just walk

43:24out and they take a lot of their clients

43:26with them right that you know the you

43:28know as people used to say about Goldman

43:31Sachs our assets go up and down in the

43:33elevators every day and to a certain

43:35degree that is true of of all banks you

43:39know e even like a a traditional like

43:41branch banking bank and you go through

43:44the disruption of a merger, all of that

43:46is at risk, you know. So, that's a big

43:50problem. Also, regulation in banking is

43:53so much stricter than every other

43:54industry. So, you got the regulators on

43:56the top, less so now under Trump, but

43:57you have the regulators on the back

43:58while you're on your back while you're

44:00trying to do it. Uh, that's another

44:03issue. And, you know, culture is really

44:06important in banking. You know, vibes,

44:08you know, it's it's a it's a people

44:10business again. So, you mess with that

44:11corporate culture and you got an issue.

44:12So, it's just really hard to do. not say

44:14it won't happen. I just like the more I

44:16talk to bankers about this process of

44:19consolidation, the harder it looks to me

44:20from the outside.

61:45being more c becoming more cautious

61:46about this narrative. And of course the

61:49dream scenario what we should be all

61:51happening is that we have kind of

61:53incremental caution rather than a moment

61:56of freakout that this kind of just cools

61:58down. A and you know look as somebody as

62:02a really smart investor

62:05Patrick Kaser at Brandy Wine Global put

62:07it to me this week. He was talking about

62:10the moment you're worried about is not

62:12that revenue that like AI data center

62:16spending just suddenly stops in the

62:18middle of 2026.

62:19That's not going to happen. The the the

62:22cranes are already there. The purchases

62:25are made. The cement is rolling. The

62:27moment you're waiting for is some moment

62:28in 2026 when people look out to 2027 or

62:322028 and they can see the end of the

62:35runway out there. Right now the road

62:38just goes on forever, right? But the

62:41moment we're waiting for because the

62:42market discounts forward. The market

62:43doesn't care what's happening today,

62:45right? So at some point the worry is

62:48that you see the end of the runway

62:50sometime in 2026.

62:53And I I just feel like uh I I don't

62:57know. My guess is no. It's not it's not

62:59a high probability prediction, but I am

63:01reassured by the fact that the market is

63:04already exercising a little bit of

63:05discipline. It is interesting the

63:07discipline that we're seeing in the

63:08markets. And I I think one of my one of

63:11my predictions for AI is that the the

63:15winners in AI are going to be the ones

63:17that can signal a level of

63:19responsibility. I mean, if you look back

63:22at the previous couple of years in AI,

63:24you were rewarded for being unrealistic.

63:28You were rewarded for saying, "We have

63:30this gigantic idea. It's going to be

63:32artificial general intelligence. We're

63:33going to take over the world." and then

63:35people gave you money for saying that. I

63:37think that it's beginning to flip now

63:40where if you start talking with that

63:42sort of technobabble language, you begin

63:44to get punished and it's the companies

63:46that are signaling the level of

63:48responsibility and usually signaling

63:50that means you are actually being

63:51responsible. And so a company that I

63:53think would be rewarded as an example

63:55would be anthropic this year versus

63:57OpenAI.

67:53good. there. This these are just stocks

67:58much to the underlying economic growth.

68:02construction company. You know, if the

68:05economy is shrinking, put the cranes

68:06away, nobody's calling. And if it's

68:08growing, you can you can price any way

68:11you want. So you're going to be you're

68:13going to be like the economy plus or the

71:08that I'm I'm but I will admit that this

71:46>> And I just wanted to push back against

71:47that narrative.

71:48>> I think it's a really important point

71:50and I think it's sort of an important

71:52reminder of how the Fed actually works.

71:55And and for those who don't know, there

71:59is a committee and each person on the

72:02committee gets one vote including the

72:05Fed chair. So the question is what power

72:10does the Fed chair really have? And you

72:13know there's power in the sense that

72:15they set the agenda for the committee.

72:18They decide which things are going to be

72:20talked about and how the conversation is

72:22going to proceed. They're also the

72:24person who gets on the on the TV and

72:27talks to the country about what is

72:29happening. But it is a really great

72:31point. It's like but also they just have

72:33one vote. There's no doubt that the way

72:36central banking works is less through

72:39the actual impact of the interest rate

72:41and more through signaling and

72:43messaging. you know, the the the the

72:46actual interest rate, we're talking

72:48about quarter point increments at a rate

72:51that very few people in the economy pay,

72:54right? Uh what's important and so where

72:58the chair can have sway is everybody on

73:00that committee knows that they're

73:02sending a message as a group to the

73:04world, right? So there is pressure for

73:07them to you know one or two dissents are

73:10okay but the world does not need a split

73:13committee from the Fed and the committee

73:16is the the open market committee is

73:18aware of that. So you know we're going

73:20to have an interesting experiment in

73:22kind of group dynamics here that we

73:25haven't had in many decades.

73:27>> Such an interesting point. I I I really

73:29hadn't considered that and I love it

73:31because it's such an obvious point.

73:33>> Yeah.
